one_line_takeaway: XMARK improves multi-bit LLM watermark decoding accuracy under limited-token conditions by intersecting green lists from multiple vocabulary permutations (evergreen list) and decoding with a count-capped constrained token-shard mapping matrix (cTMM), beating MPAC/RSBH/StealthInk/DepthW/CycleShift on bit accuracy across text completion, summarization, story generation, and machine translation while keeping perplexity comparable or better.
key_finding: Table 1 (b=8, T in {150,200,250,300}): XMARK achieves the highest average BA on all three tasks -- 99.38% (text completion, vs. 97.13% for the next-best CycleShift, 95.63% for MPAC), 82.75% (text summarization, +5.81 pts over second-best MPAC's 76.94%), and 80.94% (story generation, +6.81 pts over MPAC's 74.13%) -- while keeping average PPL comparable to or better than MPAC/RSBH/CycleShift/DepthW (e.g., 4.61 vs. MPAC's 5.08 on text completion). Table 4 (default setting, b=8): XMARK's decoding time is 0.08s, comparable to MPAC's fastest-baseline 0.06s, versus DepthW's 16.16s and RSBH's 8.45s, while achieving 98.75% BA vs. MPAC's 94.00%. Table 2 (WMT14 De-En MT, b=16): XMARK gets the best BLEU (44.52 vs. MPAC's 43.21) and best BA (64.62% vs. MPAC's 60.88%). Table 3 (long-message setting b=64, T=1000): XMARK's BA is 93.00% vs. MPAC's 85.81% and RSBH's 84.47%, with the best BSc./R-1/R-Lsum among the three. Figure 4 shows XMARK stays ahead of MPAC/RSBH/StealthInk as message length b grows to 32 (82.62% BA at b=32 on text completion, +4.62 pts over MPAC). Ablation (Table 5, text completion, b=32): increasing k from 1 (=LOSO) to 4 raises average BA from 77.72% to 88.16% at the cost of rising PPL (4.46 -> 5.11); the cTMM decoder (XMARK) consistently beats the naive TMM variant (XMARK-) at each k. Robustness (Figure 6): under Copy-Paste and Paraphrase text-editing attacks, XMARK is comparable to but not clearly better than MPAC -- the paper notes MPAC's small fixed green-list ratio (0.25) gives it an inherently stronger raw watermark signal that partly offsets XMARK's other advantages under these specific evasion attacks.
core_contribution: XMARK is a multi-bit watermarking scheme for LLM-generated text that embeds a binary message (e.g., a user ID) into generated tokens for later attribution/provenance verification. Its two novel components, both introduced by this paper, are: (1) Leave-one-Shard-out (LOSO) encoding, which flips the standard green-list construction (used by prior work MPAC) by marking ALL vocabulary shards except the one indexed by the message-block value as the boosted 'green' set, giving a much larger green-list ratio (gamma = 1 - 2^-d, >=0.75 for d>=2) than MPAC's gamma = 2^-d (<=0.25), thereby reducing text-quality distortion; and (2) an 'evergreen list' formed by intersecting k independent LOSO green lists (each built from a distinct hash key/vocabulary permutation), decoded with a constrained token-shard mapping matrix (cTMM) that caps each generated token's contribution to at most one count per shard (across all k permutations) rather than letting it count up to k times as a naive TMM would. The paper proves E[gamma] = (1 - 2^-d)^k for the evergreen list (Theorem 1, proof in appendix) and shows empirically that XMARK improves decoding bit accuracy over five baselines while keeping perplexity comparable to or better than them, especially under short generated-text (low-token) conditions.
threat_model: The setting (Section 2.1) is a cloud-hosted LLM-as-a-Service provider that stamps each generated response with a hidden b-bit message tied to identifying metadata (user ID, timestamp) so that, given a later suspect text, the provider can decode the message and attribute/trace misuse (e.g., generation of fake news, phishing emails, fraudulent reviews) back to the originating account. The adversary is a user who received watermarked text and wants to evade attribution; the paper evaluates two concrete evasion attacks on XMARK and baselines: a Copy-Paste attack (interleaving unwatermarked human text into the watermarked output at varying proportion Delta) and a Paraphrase attack using the Dipper paraphraser. It does not model attacks on the LLM provider itself, key-extraction/spoofing attacks, or model-stealing; robustness is evaluated only for text-editing evasion of the watermark signal.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states: (1) increasing block size d beyond the fixed d=2 used in experiments would expand the number of shard candidates per block, reducing observations-per-shard for fixed k and T and degrading decoding accuracy, so adapting XMARK to larger d (which would otherwise improve quality per Remark 1) is left as future work; (2) XMARK's design does not explicitly incorporate mechanisms to enhance robustness against text-editing attacks despite showing empirical robustness in evaluation; (3) the hyperparameter k trades off quality vs. accuracy and currently requires manual tuning rather than an adaptive selection strategy; (4) the watermark bias delta is fixed in all experiments even though it also materially affects the quality/accuracy trade-off, and jointly optimizing over delta is left to future work. Additionally (not in the authors' own limitations list but evident from the results): under the Paraphrase attack, XMARK only achieves BA 'comparable to' MPAC rather than beating it, and the paper itself calls paraphrase evasion 'a significant open challenge in the literature.'

datasets_used: WMT14 German-to-English (Bojar et al., 2014) - machine translation
quantitative_results: Text completion avg BA: XMARK 99.38% vs CycleShift 97.13%, MPAC 95.63%, RSBH 95.50%, StealthInk 89.81%, DepthW 88.69% (b=8, Table 1)
Text summarization avg BA: XMARK 82.75% vs MPAC 76.94%, StealthInk 67.19%, CycleShift 60.19%, RSBH 64.00%, DepthW 56.19% (b=8, Table 1)
Story generation avg BA: XMARK 80.94% vs MPAC 74.13%, CycleShift 68.19%, RSBH 65.88%, StealthInk 65.44%, DepthW 56.63% (b=8, Table 1)
Text completion avg PPL: XMARK 4.61 vs MPAC 5.08, RSBH 4.79, CycleShift 5.06, DepthW 4.64, StealthInk 4.13 (unwatermarked lower bound 3.97; Table 1)
Decoding runtime (Table 4): XMARK 0.08s, MPAC 0.06s, StealthInk 0.07s, RSBH 8.45s, DepthW 16.16s (encoding ~11.5s for all methods)
WMT14 MT (Table 2): XMARK BLEU 44.52 / BA 64.62% vs MPAC BLEU 43.21 / BA 60.88%, RSBH BLEU 38.96 / BA 58.62%
Long message/text setting b=64, T=1000 (Table 3): XMARK BA 93.00% vs MPAC 85.81%, RSBH 84.47%
b=32 message length on text completion (Figure 4 text): XMARK BA 82.62% vs MPAC (second-best) +4.62 pts lower

Ablation on k (Table 5, text completion b=32): LOSO (k=1) avg BA 77.72%/PPL 4.46; XMARK k=2 85.31%/4.72; k=3 86.16%/4.99; k=4 88.16%/5.11; XMARK- (naive TMM) trails XMARK at every k (e.g., k=2: 84.69% vs 85.31%)
Green-list ratio comparison: XMARK default E[gamma]~=0.5625 (d=2,k=2) vs MPAC/CycleShift 0.25, DepthW/RSBH 0.5

LOSO vs MPAC baseline comparison (Figure 2, T=500): LOSO BA 91.38% vs MPAC BA 95.12% (3.74-pt gap), widening to 9.50 pts at T=100, illustrating the quality/accuracy trade-off motivating the evergreen-list design
